versionNotes: Sprint 4 W13 Look-back 1 (Problem Selection) — extra-credit meta-reflection form activity with optional AI sparring partner on sections 2-5
title: Look-back 1: Problem Selection
description: Revisit your Sprint 1 or Sprint 2 problem/partner-need selection as a judgment call — not whether it was right, but how you made it and what that reveals.

questions.infoBlock:
Step 1 of 6 · Open the original

Before answering, go back and re-read your original problem selection or partner-need analysis from Sprint 1 or Sprint 2. Read it as if someone else wrote it.

🔗 Open S1: Problem Analysis (5 Whys) →🔗 Open S2: Goal Setting Part II — Partner Goals →

Pick whichever one feels more honest to revisit. Read the whole thing before continuing.
questions.prompt: Open your original submission above. Read the whole thing before continuing.

questions.sectionTitle: Step 2: What Other Options Were You Considering?
questions.infoBlock:
Step 2 of 6 · The alternatives

Think back to when you were choosing your problem or partner need. What other directions were on the table?

What other problems or needs were you seriously considering before you made your choice? List them and briefly describe each.
questions.prompt: What other problems or needs were you seriously considering before you made your choice? List them and briefly describe each.
questions.placeholder: Describe at least 2-3 alternatives you were weighing...

questions.sectionTitle: Step 3: Why Did You Rule Them Out?
questions.infoBlock:
Step 3 of 6 · The rejections

Every choice is also a rejection. What made you say no to the alternatives?

For each alternative you listed above, why did you rule it out? What reasoning or feeling drove that decision?
questions.prompt: For each alternative you listed above, why did you rule it out? What reasoning or feeling drove that decision?
questions.placeholder: Be honest about your reasoning — was it evidence, gut feeling, convenience, or something else?

questions.sectionTitle: Step 4: What Made This Harder Than It Looked?
questions.infoBlock:
Step 4 of 6 · The hidden complexity

Selection decisions often seem simple in retrospect but were messy in the moment.

What made this choice genuinely difficult? What tensions, trade-offs, or uncertainties were you navigating that aren't visible in your final submission?
questions.prompt: What made this choice genuinely difficult? What tensions, trade-offs, or uncertainties were you navigating that aren't visible in your final submission?
questions.placeholder: Describe the complexity that the clean submission hides...

questions.infoBlock:
Step 5 of 6 · The honest ratio

Sometimes we choose something because it's the best option. Sometimes we choose it because we're avoiding a harder one.

Looking back honestly: did you select your problem/need because it was the strongest choice, or partly because the alternatives felt harder, riskier, or less comfortable? What's the honest ratio?
questions.prompt: Looking back honestly: did you select your problem/need because it was the strongest choice, or partly because the alternatives felt harder, riskier, or less comfortable? What's the honest ratio?
questions.placeholder: This isn't about being wrong — it's about understanding your decision pattern...

questions.sectionTitle: Step 6: What Does This Tell You?
questions.infoBlock:
Step 6 of 6 · Closing reflection

What does this look-back reveal about how you make selection decisions? One sentence.

This is yours alone — no AI help on this one.
questions.prompt: What does this look-back reveal about how you make selection decisions? One sentence.
